extractCollocateFromIdentity uses collocatePos as a 1-based XML lemma-attribute count (via extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition) and as a 1-based whitespace-split word index (via extractPlainTextTokenAt). The comment claims 'both methods share the same 1-based token-position semantics', but this equivalence holds only when every XML token contributes exactly one lemma attribute — a non-obvious structural assumption about the identity string format that is not enforced or tested.
buildBcqlWithLemmaPrepended has two unambiguous branches: pattern starts with '[' → format and return; else → throw. Clean and exhaustive.

identifyCommonCollocates caps threshold: 'int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seedCount)' — prevents the impossible condition where minShared > seedCount from silently returning an empty set without explanation.

JSpecify annotations (@NonNull, @Nullable) are used consistently on public API surfaces: QueryExecutor, CollocateQueryPort, SketchQueryPort, ExplorationService, RelationConfig, RelationUtils all annotate every parameter and return type

CollocateResult record marks rawXml and collocateLemma as @Nullable; RelationUtils.buildFullPattern(RelationConfig, @Nullable String headword) explicitly accepts and documents null passthrough

WordSketchResult record (String lemma, String pos, ...) carries no nullability annotations on lemma or pos, despite the UNKNOWN_POS sentinel constant suggesting these fields are never null — the contract is implicit rather than expressed in annotations
CollocateEntry API response record (String lemma, long frequency, double logDice, String pos) similarly lacks nullability annotations on lemma and pos at the serialization boundary

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed: parameter is annotated @NonNull Set<String> seeds but the first body statement is Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, "seeds must not be null") — the annotation and the defensive check carry contradictory trust assumptions

SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest uses the raw string literal "dep".equals(parts[1]) for URL path routing instead of RelationType.DEP.label() — if RelationType.DEP.label() were ever changed, this route would silently stop matching
PosGroup enum has a null-safe fromString() that returns OTHER for null input; RelationType enum exposes label() as the canonical JSON value — enum discipline is generally good across the codebase

RelationEntry and RelationListEntry API response records store relationType as String (not RelationType enum), deliberately downtyping at the JSON serialization boundary; this is an explicit design choice documented via RelationType.label()

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The codebase has a well-considered layered structure: an explicit SPI boundary for exploration, narrow query ports at handler injection sites, no dependency cycles, and encapsulated internal exploration helpers. The intended dependency direction is clear and consistently followed everywhere except one place: HttpApiUtils imports ExplorationException from the exploration implementation package rather than through the spi boundary, which contradicts the package-info contract and means the catch-all error-handling wrapper has a specific subsystem dependency.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: This scores above average because the architectural intent is clear, realised in most places, and the violation is narrow — one import in one file. However, that file is the shared error-handling wrapper used by every registered route, so the blast radius of adding a second subsystem exception would be felt immediately. The open issue confirms it is a genuine boundary violation, not a stylistic preference. A near-clean architecture would resolve the exception placement; the current state is good but not tight.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a principled, capability-aligned decomposition: each package has a stated purpose, a consistent abstraction level, and predictable change ownership. The SPI boundary for exploration, narrow query ports, and the handler+assembler pattern are applied consistently across all major areas, giving a new engineer reliable mental models for navigation and extension. The only genuine roughness is the placement of ExploreResponseAssembler in api/ (a pure translation class with no HTTP lifecycle state), which is deliberately consistent with SketchResponseAssembler and explicitly covered by the api/ package-info, reducing it from a structural flaw to a mild conceptual blur.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: At 83.0, this sits solidly above the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor. The main capabilities — HTTP handling, exploration algorithms, corpus querying, domain model — each live in predictable packages, and the SPI + narrow-port patterns show deliberate architectural investment in keeping the HTTP layer decoupled from internals. The score stops short of 85 because ExploreResponseAssembler's placement (confirmed open issue) persists as a minor but real ownership signal — a pure translator in the HTTP package — and the viz/ single-file package and the wide config/ scope represent small structural oddities that a developer will notice when orienting.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase applies Java naming and structural conventions with high consistency: all handlers share the same shape, package-info.java is ubiquitous, and the api/model package has a well-established one-record-per-file pattern. Two residual nested public records (CollocateProfileEntry, ExampleEntry) break the dominant api/model convention, and the spi sub-package discipline was applied only to exploration's ExplorationService while query's analogous port interfaces remain flat alongside their implementation.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: No naming drift, no style islands, and handler sibling conventions are exemplary — these anchor the score well above the midpoint. The two nested public records in api/model are a genuine but narrow inconsistency (2 out of 11 entry types), and the spi sub-package asymmetry between exploration and query is a mild structural inconsistency that doesn't impede navigation. Together these pull the score to the low-70s rather than the high-80s.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has a clearly intentional, layered error architecture: the query layer surfaces checked IOException, the exploration layer bridges to an unchecked ExplorationException, and the HTTP boundary centralises all mapping via wrapWithErrorHandling. Exception wrapping consistently preserves cause chains throughout, and the GrammarConfigLoader two-tier strategy is explicitly documented. The main structural weakness is that SketchHandlers returns HTTP 200 for partial sketch failures (per-relation errors collected into a warnings array) while all other handlers propagate, creating a meaningful asymmetry at the HTTP surface; additionally the generic catch(Exception) catch-all in wrapWithErrorHandling conflates programming errors with expected server failures.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The architecture is deliberately designed rather than accidental — documented asymmetries, consistent cause-preserving wraps, and a clean layer separation all point to a developer who thought about error propagation. This places it above the midpoint. However, the HTTP 200/partial asymmetry makes callers responsible for checking a response body field rather than an HTTP status code, and the generic Exception catch-all masks bugs; these are real inconsistencies from a developer-experience perspective that prevent a higher score.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong naming discipline overall: domain vocabulary is used consistently across packages, method names are action-oriented and specific, and internal data-carrying types are named for their role in the pipeline rather than their structure. The three outstanding issues share a common root — naming precision slips at internal/private scope where the cost of a bad name feels lower, but the gap between the two public interface methods is the most visible problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Strong majority of names clearly communicate intent with correct domain vocabulary; the codebase would score higher if not for the asymmetric interface-method grammar (module-visible), the opaque GroupStats private record, and the misleading collocateFrequency local variable in a domain where 'frequency' has a precise technical meaning. Score reflects good-but-not-great: most names are right, a few have real ambiguity, none are egregiously generic or misleading at the public API level.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The codebase shows genuine abstraction discipline in its query layer: CollocateQueryPort/SketchQueryPort narrow handler dependencies to exactly what each handler needs, and CollocateQueryHelper encapsulates a non-trivial ranking pipeline shared across multiple methods. The one-implementation interfaces (ExplorationService, QueryExecutor) are honestly documented and serve legitimate isolation or testability purposes. The main weakness is ExploreResponseAssembler — a 285-line static-methods-only final class that bundles edge-building, response assembly, and entry conversion for all exploration endpoints into a single flat namespace, making it harder to locate and extend individual conversion paths.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: This scores above midpoint because the foundational abstractions (port splitting, exploration sub-layer decomposition, package-private helpers) are well-constructed and provide real leverage with low indirection cost. The score is held below 80 by ExploreResponseAssembler's static utility pattern, which is a cohesive but inflexible design, and by residual stale documentation pointing to the now-merged RelationPatternUtils — evidence that a prior split was cleaned up but not fully completed. No delegation-heavy classes, no facade re-exports, and no unnecessary type indirection; the remaining issues are module-scoped rather than codebase-wide.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The dependency footprint is impressively lean for a corpus-analysis tool: six production runtime dependencies, no duplicate libraries, no heavy pull-ins for trivial use, and all test/annotation deps correctly scoped. The dominant health problem is the BlackLab 4.x distribution gap — the library is not on Maven Central, so every developer and CI pipeline must execute a manual pre-build install script or rely on a raw GitHub URL with no SLA. This single issue creates meaningful onboarding friction and CI brittleness that overshadows an otherwise clean picture.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The codebase sits well above a 'significant drag' (60) baseline on most dependency-health axes — lean set, no duplicates, no over-engineering — but the BlackLab distribution issue is a systemic, not cosmetic, problem: standard Maven toolchain fails without a manual bootstrap step, which is the kind of issue the dimension specifically targets. The redundant jackson-databind version pin is minor (no conflict) but is a known open item. Together these pull the score into the low-to-mid 60s rather than the solid 70-80 range a well-resolved dependency graph would earn.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: The module boundaries are well-structured: dedicated assembler classes centralise translation, narrow port interfaces expose minimal surfaces, and the SemanticFieldExplorer façade composes its algorithmic collaborators cleanly. The two flagged open issues — ExploreResponse.Parameters reuse in ComparisonResponse and the asymmetric primarySeeds() serialisation contract — represent genuine seam leakage rather than catastrophic design failure, but they remain unresolved and create concrete friction points for future evolution.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores above the midpoint because the dominant seam pattern is explicit, layered, and translation-centralised; a developer following the call flow encounters no tangled handoffs or scattered glue code. The score is held below the 75–80 range by the two persistent open issues: the Parameters type coupling introduces an ever-nullable field at the compare boundary, and the interface-level primarySeeds() without a @JsonProperty creates a silent serialisation asymmetry that violates the principle of explicit, unsurprising boundaries.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test suite is genuinely comprehensive and well-structured for a corpus-linguistics tool that cannot be integration-tested without a large external index: 299 tests span every module, a shared stub infrastructure decouples tests cleanly from BlackLab, and real HTTP integration tests verify the server's routing path end-to-end. The primary structural gap is that the dependency-sketch execution path — while exercised at the handler layer via stubs — has no CI-runnable test for its guard clauses or BCQL construction logic, because DependencySketchTest was permanently @Disabled instead of adopting the established Assumptions.assumeTrue() + @Tag("integration") guard pattern used everywhere else.
test_strategy.score_rationale: A 70 reflects a codebase that is materially better than the median (50): CI is running, integration tests exist at multiple layers, no snapshot overuse, no fragility patterns, and the live-index gating strategy is sound. The score falls short of the 80–85 range because one critical execution path (executeDependencyPattern with BCQL construction and queryAndRankDepCollocates) has zero CI coverage beyond a stub override at the handler layer, and a handful of stubs encode implementation details that create mild regression risk on refactors.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The HTTP handler surface is well-structured and largely consistent: all handlers share a uniform single-parameter signature, parameter names are harmonised across sibling endpoints, and error contracts are explicitly documented at every layer. The two coherence gaps are the ExplorationService options-type asymmetry (exploreByRelation vs exploreMultiSeed use different options types, with a documented rationale) and the ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method, which returns conceptually different data for SingleSeed vs MultiSeed implementations and is therefore semantically dishonest for polymorphic consumers.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A score of 74 reflects a surface that is solid and deliberate, with good documentation covering deliberate asymmetries, but where two interface design decisions—options type divergence and a polymorphically ambiguous interface method—represent genuine coherence costs that would slow a new developer down. The codebase sits above the midpoint because the issues are explained rather than silent, and positive patterns (uniform handler signatures, consistent param naming, explicit error hierarchies) strongly outnumber the defects.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase implements a deliberately unauthenticated, loopback-only access model that is explicitly documented at the server level and applied with complete uniformity across all eleven endpoints. Every handler uses the same shared input-validation utilities, the same CORS machinery with wildcard protection, and the same error-handling wrapper; the most powerful endpoint (arbitrary BCQL queries) carries additional complexity and length guards plus audit logging. There are no auth decorators on some siblings but not others, no mixed trust boundaries, and no magic permission literals.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The authorization model scores high because it is coherent, documented, and applied without exception — every handler shares the same trust boundary, the same input guards, and the same CORS controls. The only architectural caveat is that the entire design relies on the loopback bind for access control, which is a single-layer perimeter; the code explicitly warns that any bind-address change requires an external auth layer, so this is a documented constraint rather than an oversight. A score of 90 reflects strong consistency with one structural fragility (single network-layer perimeter with no fallback).

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely clean of AI-generated debt at the logic level: naming is domain-specific throughout, error handling is centralized rather than copy-pasted, and inline comments explain constraints and non-obvious decisions rather than restating code. The remaining AI debt is almost entirely concentrated in Javadoc quality: cross-referencing prose that duplicates individual method descriptions, a stale reference to a non-existent RelationPatternUtils class across two files, and occasional @implNote bloat that restates the method summary.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A score of 72 reflects a codebase with no systemic AI debt in production logic, but with identifiable Javadoc-layer accumulation (the QueryExecutor 'Method responsibilities' block, stale RelationPatternUtils references, minor docstring-on-trivial-method cases) that a developer must mentally filter while navigating key interfaces. The open forTesting() and QueryExecutor issues are real but isolated; they do not indicate a pattern of AI-generated logic or broad documentation overreach, placing the score solidly in the 70–75 range rather than below 65.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase is in a very healthy state from an incomplete-migration perspective. All internal refactorings (SemanticFieldExplorer facade extraction, port-interface narrowing, config schema migration) are fully complete and actively enforced. The only finding is a single conditional TODO in WordSketchApiServer about a speculative future HTTP server change, with no owner or deadline.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: A score of 88 reflects a dimension that is nearly defect-free: one low-severity open issue (a conditional TODO with no owner/deadline) keeps the score from the 90+ range, but there are no actual incomplete migrations, deprecated-symbol calls, old-and-new API coexistence, or compatibility shims anywhere in production code. This places the codebase well above the midpoint and close to the high end of the scale.

package_organization.dimension_character: The package structure is deliberately layered and well-documented, with every package carrying a package-info.java that articulates its boundary. The coupling direction is consistently inward (api → exploration.spi → query → model) with no bidirectional edges or affinity mismatches. Three minor thin-package cases create small navigation friction: a model/ root that holds a single shared enum (PosGroup, fan-in 4), a one-interface exploration/spi/ subdirectory, and a one-file viz/ package with a sole production consumer in api/.
package_organization.score_rationale: The overall structure earns a score in the high-70s: it would be strong (80+) if not for the three thin-package decisions that add navigation hops without measurable payoff at the current codebase size. The model/ root thinness is the most actionable (PosGroup is a borderline straggler), while exploration/spi/ is a documented intentional choice whose cost is acceptable. No flat-directory overload, no ambiguous naming, no coupling-direction failures, and no import-affinity mismatches are present, keeping the score well above the midpoint.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong discipline around initialization ordering: system-property reads are deferred to call sites rather than captured at class load, port binding is explicitly separated from object construction, and shutdown-hook ordering is managed via AtomicReference. The only non-trivial static state is JsonUtils.MAPPER (a pure, thread-safe Java object with no external dependencies) and a handful of compiled regex patterns. The one mild coupling is RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES, whose string values shadow grammar-config IDs from a JSON file; drift is caught by validateAliases() at startup but produces a warning rather than a hard failure.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This dimension scores high because the codebase has no import-time side effects, no circular init dependencies, no environment reads at class scope, and thoughtful separation of construction from I/O binding. The single identified weakness — RELATION_ALIASES string-coupling to JSON config IDs — is guarded by a validation call at startup but is not a hard failure; a missed update would only log a warning, not abort. This places the score in the high-80s to low-90s range: nearly clean, with one tightenable soft coupling.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase shows strong design intent throughout: handler classes are domain-decomposed, the response assembler pattern clearly separates presentation from domain, and the exploration facade correctly delegates focused algorithms to separate classes. The single confirmed open issue — a type-honesty gap where a boolean fallback flag is encoded as Boolean.TRUE | null in the response layer — is a local inconsistency that does not reflect systemic structural incoherence.
design_coherence.score_rationale: A score of 76 reflects clearly above-average structural coherence: functions are focused, abstractions are earned, and the patterns are applied consistently across the codebase. The one confirmed issue is modest in scope (local to a single assembler method), but the asymmetric boolean→nullable encoding is a real type-honesty problem that warrants noting. Without the open issue this would comfortably sit in the low 80s.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: Contract coherence is broadly strong: nullable returns carry @Nullable annotations, null-accepting parameters are documented and honoured, and the two-tier IOException/IllegalArgumentException exception strategy in GrammarConfigLoader is precisely specified and implemented. The two persistent weaknesses are both in the exploration/query boundary: a redundant null-guard that directly contradicts a @NonNull annotation, and a test-escape factory that creates an intentionally broken instance whose behavioural limits exist only in prose, not in the type system.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The majority of the API surface — query ports, snippet parser, config loader, exploration options — shows deliberate, consistent contracts that developers can trust. The score is held below 80 by two confirmed, still-open issues that are representative of the same root pattern: a @NonNull annotation that is not fully believed (explicit null check) and a type-system bypass for test instances with a verbal-only safety contract. Neither issue is widespread, but both affect the boundary between the exploration and query subsystems, which is a high-traffic path.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: The codebase applies guard clauses and arithmetic boundary checks consistently and correctly across its query and exploration layers, making most code paths straightforward to verify. However, three persistent logic issues reduce clarity: RuntimeException is caught and re-thrown as IOException in two places (masking programming errors as infrastructure failures), a dead null check in toHitRecord creates a false safety net (safeParts throws rather than returning null, so the sentinel branch can never execute), and the collocatePos integer relies on an implicit structural equivalence between XML attribute count and plain-text word index that is underdocumented.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: A score of 67 reflects code that is mostly correct and consistently defensive, but contains real logic issues that a developer would need to investigate rather than just trust. The dead code branch in toHitRecord actively misleads; the RuntimeException masking (present in two locations) inflates the scope of an already-acknowledged concern; and the collocatePos dual-semantic is a documented but unresolved clarity debt. These are not catastrophic but they meaningfully raise the cognitive load for anyone maintaining the query pipeline.

type_safety.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates genuine JSpecify annotation discipline across its public API surfaces — interfaces, utility methods, and key domain types are consistently annotated — and uses Optional rather than @Nullable returns where absent is a normal outcome. The main gap is at the model record layer: WordSketchResult and CollocateEntry, both high-traffic types, carry unannotated String fields for lemma and pos at the boundary between the query engine and the API layer. One recurring contract issue — @NonNull annotation paired with a redundant Objects.requireNonNull body check — shows incomplete trust in the type system annotation.
type_safety.score_rationale: A score of 71 reflects a codebase that is clearly making the right efforts with type annotations (better than a bare/unannotated codebase which would score in the 40s), but has not yet achieved full disciplined coverage. The two unresolved open issues (WordSketchResult field nullability, @NonNull/@requireNonNull double-check) and the raw string URL routing bypass are concrete, specific gaps rather than pervasive problems. The score sits below 75 because the gaps cluster around high-traffic model records that propagate through the entire query-to-API pipeline.

issues.summary: SketchHandlers returns HTTP 200 for partial sketch failures, requiring callers to inspect response body for errors
issues.suggestion: Retain the partial-results design but return HTTP 207 Multi-Status (or HTTP 500 when all relations failed) so callers can detect failure without parsing the body. issues.summary: wrapWithErrorHandling's final catch(Exception) silently maps programming errors (NPE, CCE) to HTTP 500, masking bugs

RelationPatternUtilsTest.java @DisplayName is 'RelationPatternUtils' but the class imports and tests RelationUtils — the test was updated to use the merged class but the display name was not updated.

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.x absent from Maven Central forces a manual pre-build install step and a fragile raw-GitHub-URL fallback
issues.suggestion: Mirror both blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser JARs to a stable private registry (GitHub Packages, Nexus, or Artifactory) and reference that registry in pom.xml. This eliminates the manual script dependency and the unreliable raw-GitHub URL, making standard `mvn clean package` work out of the box for all developers and CI runners.
evidence: pom.xml comment (line 113-130): 'BlackLab 4.x is NOT published to Maven Central … REQUIRED FIRST STEP: run ./install-blacklab.sh'
A `blacklab-github-fallback` Maven profile exists that points to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/… with checksumPolicy=warn — no SLA, can break on rate-limit, branch deletion, or repo rename
install-blacklab.sh downloads from GitHub releases and runs `mvn install:install-file` into the local ~/.m2 repo; any CI pipeline without this pre-step fails at dependency resolution

issues.summary: jackson-databind carries an explicit version tag that duplicates what jackson-bom already manages
issues.suggestion: Remove the <version> tag from the jackson-databind dependency declaration in pom.xml; let the imported jackson-bom manage the version. This eliminates the dual-maintenance risk and is the standard pattern when a BOM is already in use.
evidence: pom.xml dependencyManagement imports jackson-bom at 2.17.2 (lines 27-32), which already pins jackson-databind to 2.17.2
pom.xml dependencies block also declares jackson-databind with <version>${jackson.version}</version> = 2.17.2 (lines 54-56)
Versions currently agree, so there is no runtime conflict, but the redundant pin means a future BOM upgrade will silently be shadowed by the explicit version unless both are updated in sync

issues.summary: ComparisonResponse reuses ExploreResponse.Parameters, inheriting a compare-inapplicable nouns_per field
issues.suggestion: Extract a shared ParametersBase record (relation, top, min_shared, min_logdice) that both ExploreResponse.Parameters and a new CompareParameters record extend or embed. ComparisonResponse should reference CompareParameters, which omits nouns_per entirely, removing the nullable placeholder from the compare seam.
evidence: ComparisonResponse.parameters is typed as ExploreResponse.Parameters, which includes nouns_per annotated @Nullable Integer.

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildComparisonResponse always passes null for nouns_per: new ExploreResponse.Parameters(relationType, params.topCollocates(), params.minShared(), params.logDiceThreshold(), null).
The compare endpoint never accepts or uses nouns_per, so the field is structurally inapplicable but permanently embedded at this boundary.

issues.summary: ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method carries no @JsonProperty, producing asymmetric serialisation across variants
issues.suggestion: Remove primarySeeds() from the sealed interface — it exists only to satisfy a shared data-access contract used in tests, but its serialisation semantics are variant-specific. Tests can cast to the concrete type or use the concrete accessors directly. Keeping the interface free of asymmetric serialisation obligations makes the boundary contract honest.
evidence: The interface declares List<DiscoveredNounEntry> primarySeeds() with no @JsonProperty annotation.
SingleSeed serialises the component accessor as discovered_nouns via @JsonProperty("discovered_nouns") on the record component.

MultiSeed suppresses the interface method with @JsonIgnore and exposes source_seeds via the separate sourceSeeds() accessor — a two-step workaround that relies on a @JsonIgnore to prevent a phantom discovered_nouns key in the multi-seed output.

A consumer iterating polymorphically over ExploreResponse values and invoking Jackson serialisation on the interface type would produce inconsistent or missing JSON keys without reading the per-variant annotations.

issues.summary: CROSS_RELATIONAL sentinel string lives in ExplorationHandlers rather than the config/domain layer
issues.suggestion: Add a CROSS_RELATIONAL constant to RelationType (or RelationUtils) and reference it from ExplorationHandlers. This makes the sentinel part of the shared vocabulary and removes the ad-hoc string from the handler layer.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers defines private static final String CROSS_RELATIONAL = "cross_relational" and passes it as the relationType argument to buildComparisonResponse.

RelationType and RelationUtils contain no corresponding constant or entry for cross-relational aggregation, so this sentinel is invisible to the domain layer and to callers that follow the config abstraction.

issues.summary: DependencySketchTest uses @Disabled instead of the established guard pattern, leaving executeDependencyPattern untested in CI
issues.suggestion: Remove @Disabled from DependencySketchTest and replace it with Assumptions.assumeTrue(INDEX_PATH != null && new File(INDEX_PATH).exists(), ...) per the pattern in BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest. Additionally, add stub-based guard-clause tests in BlackLabQueryExecutorTest (or a new DependencyPatternGuardTest) that exercise executeDependencyPattern with a null-index executor: null deprel should throw NullPointerException (Objects.requireNonNull), empty lemma should return empty list, and a valid call with a null headPosConstraint vs non-null headPosConstraint should produce distinct BCQL strings (testable by subclassing BlackLabQueryExecutor and capturing the bcql argument).
evidence: DependencySketchTest annotates the entire class with @Disabled("Requires a live BlackLab index — set CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var and remove @Disabled to run") rather than using Assumptions.assumeTrue() + @Tag("integration") as done in BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest and BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest.

BlackLabQueryExecutorTest adds CI-runnable stub-based guard tests for executeCollocations (null/empty lemma → empty list, null pattern → IAE) but no equivalent tests exist for executeDependencyPattern's guard clauses (empty lemma early-return at line 186, Objects.requireNonNull for deprel).

The headPosConstraint branch in executeDependencyPattern (two distinct BCQL formats with different regex escaping paths) and the full queryAndRankDepCollocates → collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups pipeline have no CI coverage.

issues.summary: ExplorationService sibling methods use different options types without symmetric justification at the call site
issues.suggestion: No code change is strictly required given the existing documentation is clear. If reducing API surprise is desired, consider overloading ExplorationService.exploreByRelation to accept a plain ExplorationOptions and resolve the default reverseExpansionLimit internally, so all three methods share the same options base type at their call sites.
evidence: exploreByRelation accepts SingleSeedExplorationOptions(ExplorationOptions base, int reverseExpansionLimit) while exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles accept ExplorationOptions directly.

The rationale (reverseExpansionLimit is single-seed-only) is documented in SingleSeedExplorationOptions and the interface Javadoc, but a caller discovering the interface cold must read two files to understand why the three sibling methods have non-parallel signatures.
The open finding status confirms this was previously flagged and remains unresolved.

issues.summary: ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method returns semantically different data per implementation
issues.suggestion: Remove primarySeeds() from the ExploreResponse sealed interface (it is not consumed polymorphically — ExploreResponseAssembler always constructs concrete types and HttpApiUtils accepts Object). The @JsonIgnore override in MultiSeed is a symptom: if the method were truly part of the shared contract it would not need to be ignored in one variant. Keeping the concrete discoveredNouns() and sourceSeeds() accessors on their respective records preserves type safety without the misleading shared method.
evidence: The sealed interface declares `List<DiscoveredNounEntry> primarySeeds()`. In SingleSeed it returns reverse-discovered nouns (serialised as `discovered_nouns`). In MultiSeed it returns caller-supplied seed words (serialised as `source_seeds`) and is annotated @JsonIgnore.
A developer calling `exploreResponse.primarySeeds()` polymorphically gets reverse-discovered nouns for one variant and input seeds for the other — opposing concepts sharing one method name.
The MultiSeed Javadoc acknowledges the divergence, but the interface method is still present and callable, making the interface contract misleading.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor class-level 'Method responsibilities' block duplicates individual method Javadocs
issues.suggestion: Remove the 'Method responsibilities' <ul> from the class-level Javadoc. The interface-level comment should explain the design rationale (why two ports exist, who should declare which) without restating per-method behaviour that the method Javadocs cover. The cross-method distinction notes belong inside the individual method Javadocs where they already appear.
evidence:
The interface Javadoc contains a '

Method responsibilities

'
    that describes all four methods, including distinguishing notes ('Distinct from executeCqlQuery') that are already present in the respective method Javadocs.
executeCqlQuery is described at class level ('Returns raw KWIC results without ranking') and again in its own Javadoc and @implNote, so a developer reads the same information in three places.

issues.summary: Two Javadocs reference non-existent RelationPatternUtils class after apparent rename/merge
issues.suggestion: Replace both 'RelationPatternUtils' references with '{@link RelationUtils}'. In RelationConfig, update the Javadoc to reference the actual class. In QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern, update the @param cross-reference or remove the 'typically by the caller via ...' clause if it no longer accurately describes the usage.
evidence: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern @param bcqlPattern: 'typically by the caller via RelationPatternUtils#buildFullPattern' — RelationPatternUtils does not exist; the utility lives in RelationUtils.
RelationConfig class Javadoc: 'Pattern-building and POS-group inference logic lives in RelationPatternUtils' — same ghost reference. The logic actually lives in RelationUtils.

issues.summary: Conditional TODO about migrating HTTP server to Undertow/Jetty has no owner or deadline
issues.suggestion: Either remove the TODO entirely (the current com.sun.net.httpserver choice is well-justified and the comment above already explains it), or convert it to a decision record or issue tracker item with an explicit owner if the scenario is genuinely anticipated. Do not leave it as an ownerless TODO.
evidence: Line 54: '// TODO: migrate to Undertow or Jetty if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed.'
The trigger condition ('if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed') is speculative and may never apply; no owner is named and no deadline exists

issues.summary: model/ root holds only PosGroup (fan-in 4), thinning the package below useful navigability
issues.suggestion: Option A (minimal): move PosGroup into model/sketch/ alongside WordSketchResult/CollocateResult, since POS groups are primarily a query/sketch concept, and update 4 import sites. Option B (more explicit): create model/common/ or model/core/ for cross-cutting domain enums and move PosGroup there. Staged plan: (1) git mv model/PosGroup.java model/sketch/PosGroup.java; (2) update package declaration; (3) fix imports in RelationConfig, RelationUtils, CollocateProfileComparator, SemanticFieldExplorer with sed -i 's/word_sketch.model.PosGroup/word_sketch.model.sketch.PosGroup/g' <files>; (4) run mvn test to validate.
evidence: model/PosGroup.java is the only non-package-info file in model/; 4 importers span 2 packages (config and exploration)
Fan-in = 4, just below the 5-importer threshold for a shared root type
model/package-info.java documents the sub-package rationale but does not explain why PosGroup stays at the root rather than moving to model/sketch/ or a dedicated model/common/ package

issues.summary: viz/ is a single-file package whose sole production consumer is api/VisualizationHandlers
issues.suggestion: If RadialPlot is not expected to grow into a larger visualization subsystem, collapse it into api/ as a package-private rendering helper, or move it under api/viz/ to keep the SVG-generation boundary visible without a top-level package. Staged plan: (1) git mv viz/RadialPlot.java api/RadialPlot.java; (2) update package declaration to pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.api; (3) update VisualizationHandlers import; (4) run mvn test. If future growth of a visualization library is planned, leave as-is and document intent in viz/package-info.java.
evidence: viz/ contains only RadialPlot.java (247 LOC) plus package-info.java
Fan-in = 1 production importer: api/VisualizationHandlers.java; no other package references it
RadialPlot generates SVG geometry; VisualizationHandlers wraps it in HTTP responses — distinct concerns, but with no other consumer the current packaging overhead exceeds the payoff

issues.summary: RELATION_ALIASES static map silently drifts from grammar-config JSON IDs on mismatch
issues.suggestion: Change validateAliases() to throw IllegalStateException (or at minimum log at ERROR and set a health-check flag) when any alias target is absent from the loaded grammar config, so the mismatch is caught at startup rather than discovered via 404s at request time.
evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES = Map.of('adj_modifier','noun_modifiers', 'adj_predicate','noun_adj_predicates', ...) — values are string literals that must match 'id' fields in the runtime-loaded relations.json

validateAliases(GrammarConfig) logs a warning when a mapped ID is absent from the config but does not throw, so a renamed relation would silently break alias resolution at request time rather than at startup

issues.summary: ExamplesContext.fallbackUsed boolean is encoded as Boolean.TRUE | null in buildExamplesResponse, making false and absent indistinguishable
issues.suggestion: Make the encoding honest: either (a) change ExamplesResponse.fallback to a plain boolean and always emit it (false when no fallback was used, true when it was), or (b) keep @Nullable Boolean but remove the boolean→null conversion by passing Boolean.FALSE directly when fallbackUsed is false, relying on @JsonInclude(NON_NULL) only for the genuinely 'not applicable' case (e.g., the exploration path) by omitting the field at call-site rather than through a false→null coercion.
evidence: ExamplesContext record declares `boolean fallbackUsed` — a plain two-valued type.
buildExamplesResponse passes `ctx.fallbackUsed() ? Boolean.TRUE : null` to ExamplesResponse — the value false is silently mapped to null rather than Boolean.FALSE.

ExamplesResponse.fallback is @Nullable Boolean with @JsonInclude(NON_NULL): when fallbackUsed is false the field is suppressed from the JSON response, making false and 'not set' indistinguishable to clients.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples always passes fallbackUsed=false, so the field is always absent on that path — the asymmetry is not exercised there, but it is exercised in ConcordanceHandlers where a true fallback is possible.

issues.summary: @NonNull seeds annotation contradicted by explicit null-check in exploreMultiSeed body
issues.suggestion: Remove the Objects.requireNonNull call — the @NonNull annotation is the authoritative contract. If defensive null-guarding is desired at this boundary, replace @NonNull with @Nullable and let the requireNonNull throw be the only enforcement, so annotation and behaviour agree.
evidence: Method signature: public @NonNull ExplorationResult exploreMultiSeed(@NonNull Set<String> seeds, ...)
First line of body: Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, "seeds must not be null");

@NonNull annotation signals to callers and static analysers that null must never be passed; the body then treats that guarantee as insufficient and re-checks it, sending conflicting signals about whether null is a real risk.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.forTesting() creates an instance with null index; type system cannot enforce the verbal-only usage contract
issues.suggestion: Introduce a narrow test-only interface or subclass that overrides only the purely computational methods used in tests, leaving index-dependent methods unimplemented/throwing UnsupportedOperationException with a clear message. Alternatively, pass the index as Optional<BlackLabIndex> or use a guard method (assertIndexAvailable()) with a descriptive IllegalStateException instead of a raw NPE, so that miscalling a test instance produces a clear error rather than an opaque NullPointerException.
evidence: Primary constructor: CollocateQueryHelper(@NonNull BlackLabIndex index) — @NonNull signals index is always valid.
Private no-arg constructor: this.index = null — directly contradicts @NonNull.
forTesting() factory: returns new CollocateQueryHelper() with index=null.

Javadoc warns 'Any method that delegates to the BlackLab index will throw NullPointerException if called on this instance'; this contract is verbal only — the type system cannot distinguish a test instance from a production one.

issues.summary: Dead null check in toHitRecord — safeParts never returns null, so the sentinel branch can never execute
issues.suggestion: Remove the dead 'if (parts == null)' branch in toHitRecord. Add a try-catch around the safeParts call in toHitRecord (or inside collectHits) that catches IllegalArgumentException, logs a debug message, and returns a null or skips the record — mirroring the pattern already used in executeCqlQuery.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper.toHitRecord line 299: 'String[] parts = BlackLabSnippetParser.safeParts(conc); if (parts == null) { return new HitRecord(…); }'
BlackLabSnippetParser.safeParts always either returns a non-null 3-element array or throws IllegalArgumentException — it has no code path that returns null.

If safeParts throws, the exception propagates uncaught through collectHits and out of executeBcqlQuery's outer try-catch (which only catches InvalidQuery), then surfaces as a 400 Bad Request in wrapWithErrorHandling — incorrect for an infrastructure/data error.
By contrast, executeCqlQuery correctly wraps the same safeParts call in its own try-catch to skip bad concordances, but the collectHits path has no equivalent handler.

issues.summary: @NonNull annotation on exploreMultiSeed seeds parameter contradicted by null-defensive body
issues.suggestion: Remove Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, ...) and rely solely on the @NonNull annotation, or remove @NonNull and keep the runtime check with a documented reason. Align the interface and implementation on the same trust model. Given the codebase uses JSpecify @NonNull consistently elsewhere without runtime duplicates, removing the redundant requireNonNull is the natural fix.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed declares @NonNull Set<String> seeds on line ~195 then immediately calls Objects.requireNonNull(seeds, "seeds must not be null") in the body — if the annotation is honoured by the toolchain, the runtime check is dead code; if the runtime check is needed, the annotation is a false promise
ExplorationService interface declares the same @NonNull seeds parameter without the runtime guard — the two representations carry different implicit trust levels for the same contract

issues.summary: WordSketchResult record fields lemma and pos carry no nullability annotations despite a documented UNKNOWN_POS sentinel
issues.suggestion: Annotate WordSketchResult fields with @NonNull String lemma and @NonNull String pos to express the invariant held at construction time, and apply the same to CollocateEntry. This closes the gap between the runtime guarantee (pos is always UNKNOWN_POS or a real tag, never null) and the type-declared contract.
evidence: WordSketchResult record: public record WordSketchResult(String lemma, String pos, ...) — both String fields unannotated; UNKNOWN_POS = "unknown" sentinel indicates pos is never null in practice but the record does not express this

CollocateEntry API response record mirrors the same gap: public record CollocateEntry(String lemma, long frequency, double logDice, String pos) — no @NonNull or @Nullable on lemma or pos at the serialization boundary
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates substitutes UNKNOWN_POS when posMap lacks an entry, confirming pos is always non-null at construction time — an invariant the record type could express

issues.summary: SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest matches URL path segment "dep" with a raw string literal instead of RelationType.DEP.label()
issues.suggestion: Replace "dep".equals(parts[1]) with RelationType.DEP.label().equals(parts[1]) to couple the routing to the canonical enum label and make future label changes safe.
evidence: SketchHandlers.java:59: if (parts.length > 1 && "dep".equals(parts[1])) — the string "dep" is the exact value of RelationType.DEP.label() but is not referenced through it
RelationType.DEP.label() is already used in the same file for other purposes (e.g. buildDepRelationEntry), making the inconsistency visible on inspection
If RelationType.DEP.label() were changed (e.g. to "dependency"), the URL routing branch would silently stop matching and all /api/sketch/{lemma}/dep requests would fall through to the wrong handler

add.header: Narrow ports correctly used at handler injection sites
add.description: ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers declare CollocateQueryPort; SketchHandlers declares SketchQueryPort. This pattern is consistently applied across the api layer and correctly limits each handler's visible surface to the query operations it actually needs.

add.header: No import cycles across packages
add.description: Dependency direction is consistently downward: api -> spi/query/model/config/utils; exploration -> query/model/config; query -> model/utils; config -> model/utils. No cycle pressure detected anywhere in the package graph.

add.header: exploration internals encapsulated via package-private classes
add.description: MultiSeedExplorer, SingleSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private, exposed only through the SemanticFieldExplorer facade. This correctly hides implementation decomposition from the api layer.

add.header: ExplorationException outside spi package breaks stated boundary contract
add.description: The exploration.spi package-info promises it is the only package the api layer should import, but ExplorationException lives in the parent exploration package. Placing it in spi (or handling it at the handler boundary) would close the gap.

add.header: SPI sub-package isolates exploration contract from api layer
add.description: exploration/spi/ExplorationService.java provides a deliberate contract boundary: the api/ layer imports only this interface, never the concrete SemanticFieldExplorer. The spi sub-package signals stable-contract intent explicitly.

add.header: Narrow query ports express minimal handler dependencies
add.description: CollocateQueryPort and SketchQueryPort let handlers declare only the corpus operations they actually use. SketchHandlers takes SketchQueryPort; ConcordanceHandlers takes CollocateQueryPort. This makes each handler's dependencies self-documenting and testable in isolation.

add.header: Handler + assembler pattern applied consistently across all API areas
add.description: Every API area (sketch, exploration, concordance) pairs a handler class (HTTP routing + parameter parsing) with a package-private assembler class (model-to-DTO translation). The split is documented in both assemblers' Javadocs, making the pattern teachable.

add.header: Package-info files on every package document scope concisely
add.description: All 9 packages carry a package-info.java with a one-sentence scope description; model/ includes multi-paragraph design rationale (DTO vs. value-with-derived-views, record vs. class reasoning). This gives new engineers a reliable orientation map.

add.header: WordSketchApiServer is a thin routing hub delegating to focused handlers
add.description: The server creates five handler instances and registers routes, then delegates all request processing. Adding a new API area means adding one handler class and one registerXxxHandler call — the change boundary is narrow and predictable.

add.header: model/ sub-packages partition domain types by feature area
add.description: model/sketch/ and model/exploration/ cleanly separate word-sketch result types from semantic-field exploration types. The model/ package-info explains why ExplorationResult is a class rather than a record, co-locating the design rationale with the type.

add.header: Assembler classes deliberately kept package-private in api/
add.description: ExploreResponseAssembler and SketchResponseAssembler are package-private rather than public, and the api/ package-info explicitly lists 'response assembly' as part of api/'s scope. The placement is intentional co-location, not an organisational accident — though it creates mild ownership ambiguity for translation logic.

add.header: Handler sibling convention is exemplary
add.description: All five handler classes (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) follow an identical structural template — package-private, constructor-injected dependencies, void handleX(HttpExchange) throws IOException, private static final Logger. No handler deviates from this pattern.

add.header: api/model dominant pattern is one-record-per-file
add.description: Nine of eleven named entry/response types in api/model are top-level public records in their own files. This is the established convention. Two public nested records (CollocateProfileEntry, ExampleEntry) are the remaining inconsistency from an incomplete promotion refactor.

add.header: EdgeEntry duplication resolved
add.description: The previously open issue about ComparisonResponse.EdgeEntry and ExploreResponse.EdgeEntry being duplicate nested records with identical fields is fully resolved. EdgeEntry is now a single top-level public record in api/model; both response types import it.

add.header: SPI sub-package only in exploration
add.description: ExplorationService is isolated in exploration/spi/ as the only import point for the api layer, but the analogous query ports (SketchQueryPort, CollocateQueryPort) live flat in query/ alongside BlackLabQueryExecutor. The structural discipline was not applied symmetrically across sibling subsystems.

add.header: Tiered HTTP error boundary in wrapWithErrorHandling
add.description: All HTTP handlers are wrapped by a single centralized exception-to-status mapper covering five distinct exception types before a generic catch-all. The ordering is deliberate (JsonProcessingException before IOException prevents client parse errors from being misreported as server errors). This is a consistently applied, well-ordered boundary.

add.header: Cause-preserving exception wrapping throughout
add.description: Every catch-and-rethrow site inspected passes the original exception as the cause argument. No context is lost at any boundary — IOException→ExplorationException, ErrorOpeningIndex→IOException, RuntimeException→IOException all preserve the full stack.

add.header: ExplorationException as clean layer bridge
add.description: SemanticFieldExplorer wraps IOException in ExplorationException in all four public methods, keeping the exploration SPI free of checked I/O exceptions and containing raw I/O concerns within the exploration layer. The design is documented in ExplorationException's class Javadoc.

add.header: GrammarConfigLoader documented two-tier exception strategy
add.description: IOException for file/parse failures, IllegalArgumentException for structural config errors — clearly documented in the class Javadoc with a rationale distinguishing environmental failures from programmer/configuration errors. The loadUnchecked convenience wrapper further documents the IOException→IllegalStateException promotion.

add.header: SketchHandlers partial-result pattern is intentional but HTTP-asymmetric
add.description: Per-relation IOException is collected into a warnings array and returned with HTTP 200 / status='partial'. This is explicitly documented as an intentional design choice to avoid discarding successfully computed relations. The asymmetry with exploration handlers (which propagate and yield HTTP 500/503) is real but reasoned.

add.header: Generic Exception catch-all at HTTP boundary masks programming errors
add.description: The final catch(Exception e) in wrapWithErrorHandling maps NPE, ClassCastException, and other programming errors to HTTP 500, indistinguishable from legitimate unexpected I/O failures. The current code does NOT explicitly catch NullPointerException (the prior reported behaviour); NPE is caught implicitly by the generic handler.

add.header: Strong public API naming; slippage at private scope
add.description: Public interface and exported-class method names are domain-accurate and consistently formed. Naming quality degrades at private-record and local-variable scope, where three known issues live. This pattern suggests a naming culture that prioritises the public contract but does not apply the same scrutiny to internal data carriers.

add.header: frequency/freq vocabulary is corpus-frequency in this codebase
add.description: Throughout the codebase 'frequency', 'freq', and 'Freq' consistently refer to corpus occurrence count (getTotalFrequency, headwordFreq, collocateFreq, WordSketchResult.frequency). Any local variable named *frequency that holds a count of something else (e.g. noun-sharing count) breaks this convention and should be renamed.

add.header: CollocateQueryPort/SketchQueryPort narrow handler dependencies
add.description: ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers declare CollocateQueryPort; SketchHandlers declares SketchQueryPort. Each handler receives only the methods it calls — real abstraction leverage, not interface proliferation.

add.header: SharedExploreParams translation layer eliminated
add.description: The previously flagged SharedExploreParams record no longer exists; ExplorationHandlers builds ExplorationOptions directly. The api/domain duplication issue is resolved.

add.header: RelationUtils/RelationPatternUtils merged into one class
add.description: The two static-utility classes flagged in the prior review were consolidated into RelationUtils, removing the parallel-class split. Stale javadoc references remain in RelationConfig and QueryExecutor pointing to the now-deleted class.

add.header: Static utility pattern used for presentation assemblers
add.description: ExploreResponseAssembler and SketchResponseAssembler are final classes with only static methods. This choice centralises presentation logic but prevents dependency injection, subclassing, and per-response-shape narrowing.

add.header: Lean production runtime dependency set
add.description: Only six production runtime JARs are declared: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, jackson-databind, slf4j-api, logback-classic, and log4j-to-slf4j. No duplicate libraries, no heavy pull-ins for marginal use, and every dep is actively used in production code.

add.header: log4j-to-slf4j bridge correctly scoped
add.description: BlackLab brings a log4j dependency; the project bridges it through SLF4J via log4j-to-slf4j without pulling a full log4j runtime. This keeps the logging stack unified and avoids classpath conflicts.

add.header: jspecify annotations in provided scope
add.description: org.jspecify:jspecify is declared with scope=provided, so nullability annotations are checked at compile time but contribute zero bytes to the shaded JAR at runtime.

add.header: CLAUDE.md lists stale fastjson dependency
add.description: CLAUDE.md documents 'Fastjson 2.0.25' as a project dependency, but fastjson is not present in pom.xml; Jackson (jackson-databind) is the actual JSON library. The documentation is stale but there is no actual duplicate JSON library in the build.

add.header: Assembler classes centralise all model-to-DTO translation
add.description: ExploreResponseAssembler and SketchResponseAssembler each own the full model-to-presentation mapping for their endpoint families. No handler or service class builds response objects directly — all translation is funnelled through these assemblers, which keeps the seam clean and easy to find.

add.header: Narrow port interfaces minimise boundary surface
add.description: CollocateQueryPort and SketchQueryPort expose only the query operations each handler family needs. Handlers that do not require full QueryExecutor capability declare the narrower port, making their dependency footprint explicit and reducing coupling at the query boundary.

add.header: SemanticFieldExplorer composes three focused algorithm classes
add.description: SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator are each package-private and own one algorithm. SemanticFieldExplorer is a thin façade that delegates to them without tangling their logic, making orchestration read as composition rather than interleaved control flow.

add.header: Error propagation follows single consistent path across layers
add.description: IOException from the query layer is wrapped as ExplorationException by the service layer, then caught and mapped to HTTP 503 by HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling. All other exception types map to predictable status codes in one place, keeping lifecycle propagation uniform across every exploration endpoint.

add.header: CI pipeline added, runs on push/PR to main
add.description: A .github/workflows/ci.yml workflow was added that runs on push and pull_request to main (and the active health branch). Unit tests run automatically via mvn test -DexcludedGroups=integration. The previously-open 'No CI pipeline' issue is resolved.

add.header: Rich stub infrastructure decouples all tests from live index
add.description: StubQueryExecutor (shared, subclassable), MockExchangeFactory (HTTP exchange mock), and GrammarConfigHelper (test grammar loader) are well-factored shared helpers. All 299 unit tests run without a BlackLab index on disk.

add.header: Real HTTP integration tests via ephemeral server
add.description: WordSketchApiServerTest starts the server on port 0, sends real HTTP requests via HttpClient, and verifies JSON response shapes — genuine route-to-serialisation integration coverage.

add.header: DependencySketchTest permanently disabled, not gated
add.description: All 6 dependency sketch tests use @Disabled instead of the established Assumptions.assumeTrue() guard. Guard-clause and BCQL-construction logic for executeDependencyPattern have no CI coverage.

add.header: Uniform handler signature across all HTTP handlers
add.description: Every handler method takes exactly one parameter (HttpExchange) and delegates all parameter parsing to HttpApiUtils helpers. This is consistent across SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers.

add.header: Consistent seed/collocate/relation/top param vocabulary
add.description: Both /api/semantic-field/examples and /api/concordance/examples use the same four parameter names; ConcordanceHandlers Javadoc explicitly documents the alignment with the sibling endpoint, preventing future drift.

add.header: Explicit layered error contract in HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling
add.description: The error hierarchy (413 / 400 / 503 / 500) is fully documented with exception type mappings, and ExplorationService declares all possible exceptions on every method. No missing error contracts.

add.header: ExploreResponse.primarySeeds() interface method has divergent semantics
add.description: In SingleSeed it returns reverse-discovered nouns; in MultiSeed it returns caller-supplied seeds and is @JsonIgnore'd. The interface method is not used polymorphically in practice but creates a misleading shared contract.

add.header: ExplorationService options type asymmetry is documented but remains
add.description: exploreByRelation uses SingleSeedExplorationOptions while exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles use ExplorationOptions. The rationale (reverseExpansionLimit is single-seed-only) is documented across two files, but the asymmetry adds cognitive overhead at the call site.

add.header: Loopback-only bind is the sole access-control layer
add.description: The server intentionally binds to 127.0.0.1 only and documents that any change to the bind address must be accompanied by an external auth layer. This is a deliberate architectural choice for a local research tool, not an oversight — all endpoints are unauthenticated by design.

add.header: Wildcard CORS requires explicit opt-in at startup
add.description: HttpApiUtils.warnIfWildcardCors() throws IllegalStateException if cors.allow.origin=* without -Dallow.wildcard.cors=true. This is a positive safety valve that prevents accidental CORS exposure during deployment or configuration changes.

add.header: Arbitrary BCQL endpoint has audit logging and complexity limits
add.description: POST /api/bcql logs source address, query length, and full query text on every call, and enforces pattern-length and bracket-depth limits. The elevated risk of a free-form query interface is addressed consistently relative to its sibling endpoints.

add.header: Input validation uniformly applied via shared HttpApiUtils
add.description: All handlers delegate to requireParam(), parseIntParam(), parseDoubleParam(), and MAX_PARAM_LENGTH; no handler bypasses these guards. This makes the trust boundary consistent across all siblings.

add.header: Production logic is free of AI hallmarks
add.description: All domain identifiers use corpus-specific terms (executeCollocations, buildAndRankCollocates, findCollocateIntersection). Inline comments explain constraints and non-obvious decisions, not code mechanics. Error handling is centralized, not copy-pasted. These are strong positive signals that logic-level AI debt is absent.

add.header: Stale RelationPatternUtils ghost references in two Javadocs
add.description: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern and RelationConfig both reference a RelationPatternUtils class that does not exist. The logic was merged into RelationUtils during refactoring but the Javadocs were not updated. This is a localised documentation-rot artifact, not a logic debt.

add.header: warnIfWildcardCors Javadoc issue resolved
add.description: The previously flagged 'multi-paragraph restating Javadoc' on warnIfWildcardCors has been trimmed to a single-paragraph opt-in explanation plus @throws. The current version is concise and non-restating.

add.header: Config schema migration actively enforced at load time
add.description: GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys() rejects the deprecated 'copulas' top-level key with a clear error message. The past config migration is complete and cannot regress — any old-format config fails fast on startup.

add.header: SemanticFieldExplorer facade extraction is complete and documented
add.description: After extracting SingleSeedExplorer and MultiSeedExplorer, SemanticFieldExplorer is explicitly described as a 'thin facade' in its Javadoc. There are no leftover algorithm fragments or duplicated logic indicating a partial refactor.

add.header: Handler port-interface narrowing is consistent and complete
add.description: All API handler classes declare the narrowest port interface they actually use (CollocateQueryPort or SketchQueryPort) rather than the full QueryExecutor. This is uniform across all handlers with no regressions to the wider interface.

add.header: Layered coupling direction with no violations
add.description: Imports consistently flow api → exploration.spi → query → model with no bidirectional or upstream edges. This is maintained across all 12 packages and is a strong structural positive.

add.header: Every package has documented package-info.java
add.description: All 12 production packages carry a package-info.java with a clear statement of boundary and purpose. This is an unusually thorough practice that significantly aids navigation.

add.header: Model layer split by feature into exploration and sketch subpackages
add.description: model/exploration/ (13 files) and model/sketch/ (5 files) separate domain models by feature domain. The split is explicitly documented in model/package-info.java with rationale.

add.header: api/model/ cleanly offloads HTTP response DTOs
add.description: 13 response DTO files live in api/model/ rather than in the domain model layer, preventing wire format from polluting core domain types.

add.header: exploration/spi/ is deliberate API boundary, not accidental thin package
add.description: The spi subdirectory is documented as 'the only package the api layer needs to import' from exploration. This is an intentional dependency-inversion choice that correctly prevents api from importing exploration implementation classes.

add.header: model/ root holds only PosGroup (fan-in 4, below threshold)
add.description: The model/ root package contains only PosGroup.java. With 4 importers spread across config and exploration, it is a borderline straggler — slightly below the 5-importer threshold, but used across two distinct packages rather than owned by one.

add.header: System properties read at call time not class load
add.description: Both cors.allow.origin (HttpApiUtils.corsAllowOrigin) and grammar.config (GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish) are read on each call rather than captured in static fields, deliberately enabling test-time overrides without class reloading.

add.header: Construction separated from network binding in server
add.description: WordSketchApiServer defers port binding to start(); the constructor only wires handler objects. An AtomicReference manages shutdown-hook ordering so the executor is always closed even if server construction throws. This explicit init-ordering discipline prevents resource leaks.

add.header: No class-scope I/O or external dependency initialization
add.description: Across all 76 production Java files there are no static initializer blocks performing I/O, no DB connections, and no network calls at class-load time. Static fields are limited to Loggers, compiled Patterns, and one pure ObjectMapper singleton.

add.header: RELATION_ALIASES to JSON config soft coupling
add.description: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a hard-coded Map whose values must match 'id' fields in the runtime-loaded relations.json. Drift produces only a logged warning via validateAliases(), not a startup failure, creating a silent misconfiguration risk.

add.header: Domain-decomposed handler classes in API layer
add.description: WordSketchApiServer delegates to five focused handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers), each owning a distinct request type. This decomposition is deliberate and clean — no handler mixes concerns from another domain.

add.header: ExploreResponseAssembler centralises presentation translation
add.description: All model-to-presentation translation for exploration endpoints is centralised in ExploreResponseAssembler, explicitly keeping domain result types (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) free of JSON concerns. The pattern is consistently applied across single-seed, multi-seed, comparison, and examples response paths.

add.header: SemanticFieldExplorer as thin facade over algorithm classes
add.description: SemanticFieldExplorer is a documented facade over SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator — each owning a focused algorithm. The facade handles error translation (IOException → ExplorationException) and parameter validation without duplicating algorithmic logic.

add.header: Private inner records for typed intermediate state
add.description: BlackLabQueryExecutor uses GroupStats, ExecutedSketch, and RelationQueryBatch as private records to keep intermediate data structures typed, named, and co-located with the methods that produce them, avoiding anonymous Map/List tuple passing.

add.header: Port interfaces scope handler dependencies precisely
add.description: SketchQueryPort and CollocateQueryPort sub-interfaces allow handler classes to declare exactly the capabilities they require from the query layer, rather than depending on the full QueryExecutor contract. This is a genuine interface-honesty improvement.

add.header: Nullable return contracts consistently annotated in parser layer
add.description: BlackLabSnippetParser methods that may return null (extractPosFromMatch, safeParts, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, etc.) are all annotated @Nullable; callers perform null checks before use. This is a positive pattern that removes guesswork at call sites.

add.header: Two-tier IOException / IllegalArgumentException strategy in config layer
add.description: GrammarConfigLoader documents and implements a deliberate two-tier exception strategy: IOException for I/O failures, IllegalArgumentException for structural/validation failures. This distinction makes error handling predictable for callers.

add.header: Nullable parameter contracts honoured in executeCollocations and executeDependen
add.description: @Nullable lemma / headPosConstraint parameters are documented with explicit behaviour ('returns empty list silently', 'pass null to skip') and implementations match those contracts exactly.

add.header: Consistent guard-clause pattern at query entry points
add.description: All public query methods that accept a lemma open with a null/empty guard that short-circuits to Collections.emptyList(). This keeps the happy path visible and ensures callers never receive unexpected NullPointerExceptions from forgetting to validate upstream.

add.header: Arithmetic guards precede all LogDice calls
add.description: Every call site for LogDiceUtils.compute() is preceded by explicit positive-value guards (headwordFreq > 0, collocateFreq > 0), preventing the IllegalArgumentException the utility throws on zero inputs. This is consistent across both buildAndRankCollocates and scoreHits.

add.header: Dead null check in toHitRecord after safeParts tightening
add.description: safeParts was hardened to throw rather than return null, but toHitRecord's null-return sentinel branch was not removed. The dead branch misleads readers about error handling and leaves a gap where IllegalArgumentException from safeParts propagates as a 400 client error.

add.header: JSpecify annotations used consistently on interface surfaces
add.description: QueryExecutor, CollocateQueryPort, SketchQueryPort, ExplorationService, and key utility methods like RelationUtils.buildFullPattern all carry @NonNull/@Nullable on every parameter and return. This represents deliberate annotation discipline rather than ad-hoc coverage.

add.header: Optional used instead of @Nullable returns on query accessors
add.description: RelationConfig.relationType() wraps the nullable rawRelationType field behind Optional<RelationType>, and GrammarConfig.relation(String) returns Optional<RelationConfig>. The codebase documents this pattern explicitly in RelationConfig's Javadoc, showing intentional design.

add.header: Model record fields unannotated at query-to-API boundary
add.description: WordSketchResult and CollocateEntry are high-traffic types that lack nullability annotations on their String fields despite runtime invariants (UNKNOWN_POS sentinel) guaranteeing non-null values. The annotation layer does not reach into these core model records.

add.header: @NonNull annotation adoption is partial, leaving redundant runtime checks
add.description: The codebase has @NonNull annotations on method parameters but some methods (notably exploreMultiSeed) retain legacy Objects.requireNonNull guards from before annotations were added, creating contradictory trust signals.
